lesson.menu_title: Explain It Like I Am the CFO -- Defending a Technical Decision
lesson.menu_desc: Kill the jargon: plain words, one analogy, the business benefit -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Explain It Like I Am the CFO -- Defending a Technical Decision
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 14 -- Explain It Like I Am the CFO

plan_row.tema: Explain It Like I Am the CFO -- Defending a Technical Decision
plan_row.foco: which means + comparatives (cheaper / faster than) + analogy (it is like...)
plan_row.atividade: One-slide answer + role-play with an executive who hates jargon
plan_row.homework: Record a 90-sec explanation of a technical decision, with zero jargon

paras: Engineers lose executives in the first ten seconds, and they never know why. They start with the technology: the database, the containers, the pipeline. The executive hears three words she does not know, decides that this is not her problem, and opens her laptop.

The fix is not to speak more slowly. It is to change what you start with. Start with the money, the time or the risk -- what the company gains, or what it loses. The technology is the reason, and the reason comes second, in one short sentence.

Then, if you need one more level, use an analogy. 'The legacy database is like a car from 2010 that we keep repairing: every repair is cheap, and we do six a year.' Nobody buys the architecture. They buy the result, and the result has to be in their language.

choices: Executives are not intelligent enough to understand technical details.
Start with the business result, then give the technical reason in one sentence -- and use an analogy.
You should explain the architecture slowly, with more detail.

items: You should open with the technology, so people understand the decision.
Open with the money, the time or the risk. The technology comes second.
An analogy helps when the topic is technical.
'It is like a car from 2010 that we keep repairing.'
Executives buy the result, not the architecture.
The result has to be in their language -- money, time, risk.

bank.label: The five lines of a plain-English answer
items: In plain terms: we spend four hours every week fixing the old database.
We are replacing it, which means those four hours go back to the client.
The legacy system is like a car from 2010: every repair is cheap, and we do six a year.
The new one is cheaper to maintain, and safer for the client's data.
If we do nothing, the cost is the same four hours, every week, forever.
I need four weeks. After that, the team is faster, and the risk is lower.

items.situation: Open the answer with money or time, in one sentence.
tips: 'In plain terms, we lose four hours a week.'
No technology in the first sentence.

items.situation: Explain the technical reason -- in one sentence, no acronyms.
tips: If you use a technical word, translate it immediately.

items.situation: Give an analogy from real life.
tips: Cars, houses, kitchens -- things everybody knows.

items.situation: She says: 'The system works. Why touch it?' Answer.
tips: 'The same four hours, every week, forever.'

items.situation: She asks the price. You do not have the exact number.
tips: Give a range and a date for the exact number.

items.situation: Close in one sentence: what you need, and what she gets.
tips: 'I need four weeks. After that, the team is faster.'

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Explain it again, in one sentence, as if I were the client.
How do I know this is not just engineers who want a new toy?
What happens if I say no today?

pressure.text: Result first, reason second, analogy third. If she asks for the architecture, she will ask -- do not volunteer it.

listenings.text: Diogo, you are on the agenda for the steering committee at three. You have sixty seconds, and I want to warn you about this room. The CFO is there. She does not know what a container is, and she does not want to know. Last quarter an architect used the word Kubernetes twice and she stopped listening after the first one. So do not open with the technology. Open with money or time. Tell her what we lose today, and what we get back. If you need to explain how it works, give her an analogy, something from real life. And whatever you do, do not say the word migration. Say what changes for the client.

Thank you. In plain terms: every week, my team spends four hours keeping a fifteen-year-old system alive. Four hours that the client pays for and does not see. We are replacing that system, which means those four hours go back to client work, every week, from July. Here is an analogy. The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing it -- six times a year. At some point you are not saving money, you are just spending it slowly. The new system is cheaper to maintain, and it is safer, because the old one is out of support and it stores client data. If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours a week, forever. I need four weeks and no new features in that time. After that, the team is faster, and the risk is lower.

extra_audio.text: Sophia gives Diogo sixty seconds and asks why the project needs four weeks. Diogo opens with time: four hours every week keeping the old system alive. She asks him to justify the four weeks, and he gives the analogy of the car from 2010. He explains the benefit: cheaper to maintain, and safer for the client's data. Finally, Sophia asks for the whole answer in one slide.
